title: Semantic HTML Structure
description: Learn to use semantic HTML5 elements like <header>, <main>, <nav>, <section>, <article>, and <footer> to create meaningful page structures.
instructions: Create a webpage that uses at least 5 different semantic HTML5 elements. The page should have a proper document outline with a header containing navigation, a main content area with sections, and a footer.
starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Semantic HTML</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Add your semantic HTML here -->
  
</body>
</html>
solution:
• Home
  • About

Welcome

First Article

Content here.

Related Links

© 2024
hints: Start with <header> and <nav> for the top of your page
Use <main> to wrap your primary content
Consider using <article> for self-contained content blocks

title: Interactive Forms
description: Build accessible HTML forms with proper labels, input types, and validation attributes.
instructions: Create a registration form with fields for name, email, password, and a submit button. Use proper input types, labels, and HTML5 validation attributes like required, minlength, and pattern.
solution:
<form id="registration">
  <label for="name">Full Name</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required minlength="2">
  
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>
  
  <label for="password">Password</label>
  <input type="password" id="password" name="password" required minlength="8">
  
  <button type="submit">Register</button>
</form>
hints: Use the 'for' attribute on labels matching input 'id'
The 'email' input type provides built-in validation
Add 'required' to mandatory fields

title: Flexbox Layout Mastery
description: Master CSS Flexbox to create flexible, responsive layouts without floats or positioning hacks.
instructions: Create a navigation bar using Flexbox that distributes items evenly, and a card layout that wraps on smaller screens. Use flex-wrap, justify-content, and align-items.
hints: Use display: flex on the parent container
flex-wrap: wrap allows items to flow to new lines
gap provides spacing between flex items

title: CSS Grid Layouts
description: Use CSS Grid to create complex two-dimensional layouts with rows and columns.
instructions: Create a dashboard layout using CSS Grid with a header spanning the full width, a sidebar, main content area, and footer. Use grid-template-areas for a clear layout definition.
hints: grid-template-areas gives you a visual map of your layout
Use named areas to place items intuitively
1fr takes up remaining available space

title: Responsive Design with Media Queries
description: Create responsive designs that adapt to different screen sizes using media queries and mobile-first approach.
instructions: Convert a desktop layout to be mobile-first responsive. Start with a single-column mobile layout and add media queries for tablet (768px) and desktop (1024px) breakpoints.
hints: Start with the smallest screen size first
Use min-width media queries for mobile-first
Test at each breakpoint to ensure smooth transitions

title: DOM Manipulation Basics
description: Learn to select, create, modify, and remove DOM elements using JavaScript.
instructions: Write JavaScript to: 1) Select an element by ID, 2) Create a new list item, 3) Add it to an existing unordered list, 4) Change the text content of a heading. Use querySelector, createElement, and appendChild.
hints: querySelector uses CSS selector syntax
createElement creates a new element in memory
appendChild adds the element to the DOM

title: Event Handling & Delegation
description: Handle user interactions with event listeners and learn event delegation for dynamic content.
instructions: Create a todo list where clicking an 'Add' button adds a new item, and clicking any item toggles its completion state. Use event delegation on the list container instead of attaching listeners to each item.
hints: addEventListener attaches a handler to an element
Event delegation uses a single listener on a parent
e.target tells you which child element was clicked

title: Fetch API & Async/Await
description: Make HTTP requests using the Fetch API with async/await and handle errors gracefully.
instructions: Write an async function that fetches user data from a JSON API, handles loading states, displays the data, and catches any errors with appropriate user feedback.
hints: Always check response.ok before parsing
Use try/catch for error handling with async/await
The finally block runs whether the request succeeded or failed

title: Working with JSON Data
description: Parse, transform, and serialize JSON data for API communication and local storage.
instructions: Write functions to: 1) Parse a JSON string into an object, 2) Transform data by filtering and mapping, 3) Serialize the result back to a formatted JSON string. Handle invalid JSON gracefully.
hints: JSON.parse converts a string to an object
Always wrap JSON.parse in try/catch
JSON.stringify with null, 2 creates pretty-printed output

title: MVC Pattern in Vanilla JS
description: Implement the Model-View-Controller pattern to separate concerns in a JavaScript application.
instructions: Refactor a todo application into MVC pattern: Model handles data and logic, View handles DOM rendering, Controller coordinates between them. Each should be in its own module/class.
hints: The Model should never touch the DOM
The View should never modify data directly
The Controller is the bridge between Model and View

title: Building a REST Client
description: Create a complete REST API client with CRUD operations using fetch.
instructions: Build an API client class that supports GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE operations. Include proper headers, error handling, and response parsing. Test it against JSONPlaceholder API.
hints: Create a base request method to avoid repetition
Always set Content-Type header for POST/PUT
Use a base URL to keep endpoint calls clean

title: CSS Transitions & Animations
description: Create smooth transitions and keyframe animations to enhance user experience.
instructions: Create a card component that smoothly scales up on hover, a loading spinner using @keyframes, and a fade-in animation for page content. Use transition timing functions for polish.
hints: transition is for state changes (like hover)
@keyframes defines multi-step animations
Use 'forwards' to keep the final state after animation
